ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial enzymes are synthetic bio-catalysts engineered to mimic the intrinsic properties of natural enzymes to accelerate biological reactions by lowering their activation energy [1]. The emergence of nanotechnology paved the way for the development of a new class of artificial enzymes known as nano-enzymes (nanozymes), marking a notable breakthrough in this field. Nanozymes aim to replicate the efficiency, substrate selectivity, and specificity of natural enzymes with a broad range of biocompatibilities [2]. However, nanozymes must overcome various challenges of natural enzymes, such as restricted operational stability due to denaturation and digestion, high costs, and prolonged purification processes [3]. Following the groundbreaking and pioneering work of Yan et al. in 2007, a multitude of nanozymes based on Iron [4], Vanadium [5], noble metals [6], and Carbon [7] emerged in the ensuing years.

Integrating the enzyme mimic activity of nanomaterials and their remarkable physicochemical properties can help to create multifunctional nanozymes. Such innovations could expand the application of nanozymes across various fields, including pollutant detoxification, biosensing, drug delivery, and cancer therapy [8,9]. A nanozyme-based fluorescent sensor could be an excellent option for bio-medical sensing due to its selective self-signal response [10]. Quantum dots (QDs) stand out as an optimal choice for fluorescence nanomaterials due to their size-tunable emission, high quantum yield, and excellent photostability [11–15]. Manganese oxide nanoparticles have garnered significant attention due to their versatile physiochemical properties. Mn3O4 and MnO2 are the most renowned oxides because of their stability and broad applications. The reactivity and catalytic properties of these oxides are greatly influenced by their structural arrangements. The unique spinel structure of Mn3O4 provides a higher number of active sites, leading to improved catalytic activity compared to MnO2 [16–19]. Additionally, the oxygen redox activity and broader pH tolerance make Mn3O4 adaptable to different biological environments in contrast to MnO2 [20]. Mn3O4 nanoparticles have recently gained attention due to their nanozyme activity and have been effectively utilized for reducing oxidative stress in cellular interactions [19,21–23]. Even though there are few reports on M3O4 QDs [24,25], to the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the synthesis of luminescent Mn3O4 QDs and exploring their nanozyme-based biosensing properties.

The catechol oxidase, or polyphenol oxidase, is a group of enzymes commonly found in bio-systems, that efficiently catalyses the oxidation of ortho-diphenols into ortho-quinones[26]. Artificial catechol-oxidase mimicking nanomaterials have been the subject of only a limited number of published reports [27–30]. Monitoring catecholamine neurotransmitters in body fluids is vital in the early detection of a wide range of neurodegenerative diseases. Dopamine (DA) is a neurotransmitter as well as a hormone in the catecholamine family, which helps to trigger several cellular signalling mechanisms in the human body [31]. The excessive DA levels are known to be cardiotoxic, whereas its deficiency causes neurological dysfunctions in the human body [32]. Despite the availability of various techniques in the market, the quest for developing a straightforward, highly efficient, and cost-effective dopamine sensor remains a thriving area of research. Further, these efforts have the potential to aid drug development and to comprehend the intricate actions of the brain [33]. Among the various DA detection techniques, fluorescence-based sensing has acquired significant attraction due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, and selectivity. A variety of fluorescence sensors for DA, employing both turn-on and turn-off strategies, have been reported. These sensing mechanisms involve ground state complex formation [34], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [35], photon-induced charge transfer [36], and formation of charge transfer complex [37]. Despite their merits, these sensors were left behind due to tedious synthesis procedures, limited fluorescence stability in various physiological conditions, and poor limit of detection. In this context, the development of Mn3O4 QDs with superior fluorescence and catalytic activity can potentially serve as a candidate for a stable DA sensor, as it may provide promising sensitivity and selectivity.

This work presents a novel multifunctional Mn3O4 QDs with both fluorescence and superior catechol-oxidase mimic activity. A facile biomolecule-assisted synthesis approach is employed, which enabled the tailoring of surface characteristics of the QDs. The current approach is a bottom-up process that simplifies the conventional, tiresome, and complicated nanozyme synthesis procedure into a facile one-pot method under mild reaction conditions. A significant and innovative accomplishment of this work is the modification of Mn3O4, a conventional UV–visible absorber, into a highly stable, biocompatible luminescent material. As-prepared Mn3O4 QDs behaves as a catechol-oxidase mimic nanozyme with strong affinity and activity similar to natural catechol oxidase enzyme. This multifunctional properties positions Mn3O4 QDs as highly promising candidate for fluorescence-based detection of catecholamines. Additionally, a highly sensitive dopamine (DA) sensing nanoplatform based on a fluorescence turn-off strategy has been developed from these novel QDs, demonstrating a good recovery rate in real matrices. The kinetics of catechol-oxidase mimic activity and the charge transfer complex formation between the analyte and the QDs are currently being investigated. The consistent fluorescent turn-off capabilities of QDs in the presence of DA will actively contribute to the advancement of nanozyme-based sensor fabrication for health care applications.

Potassium Permanganate (KMnO4), L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) (L-cyst), Histidine (His), Tryptophan (Trp), Tyrosine (Try), Vitamin – C (Vit − C), Dopamine Hydrochloride, Uric acid (UA), Glycine (Gly), Aluminium Oxide (Al2O3), Ferric Chloride (FeCl3), Nickel Chloride (NiCl2), Zinc Chloride (ZnCl2), Calcium Chloride (CaCl2), Potassium Chloride (KCl), Sodium Chloride (NaCl), Cobalt Chloride (CoCl2), Disodium Hydrogen Phosphate, Sodium Dihydrogen Phosphate and Benzoylated Dialysis tubing D7884 1 kDa were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, US). D- Glucose (Glu), Aspartic acid (AA), Hydroquinone (HQ), Malic acid (MA), Succinic acid (SA), Catechol, and Thio-malic acid (TA) were acquired from SRL chemicals (Mumbai, India). 3,5-di-tert-butylcatechol (DTBC) was provided by HiMedia (Mumbai, India). All chemicals obtained were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The samples were structurally characterized using Rigaku Miniflex-600 Benchtop X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 A⁰). The scan range was set from 20 to 80⁰ (2θ) at 0.02⁰ per minute. The morphology and microstructure of the samples were studied using High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM Jeol 2100) with an operating voltage 200 kV. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra were recorded using Thermo Nicolet Avtar 370 DTGS spectrometer in the range 400-4000 cm−1. Elemental composition and electronic states of the material were analyzed by X-ray Photon spectrum (XPS) using Omicron Nanotechnology Ltd, Germany with Mg- Kα source. Horiba SZ-100 nanoparticle analyser was employed for the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements and zeta potential analysis. The absorption spectra of the prepared samples were recorded using a JASCO-V-570 UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence sensing studies were conducted using Horiba QM −8075–11-C fluorometer. The Time-resolved Fluorescence Measurements were recorded using Horiba Fluoromax with the Flourohub TCSPC system. Nano LED with a peak wavelength of 370 nm was used as an excitation source for decay studies. The redox properties of the probe and the analyte were examined in a CHI-6054E electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode set-up with a scan rate of 5 mVs−1. Glassy carbon was used as the working electrode. Platinum (Pt) wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes respectively.

The synthesis of Mn3O4 QDs was carried out through a one-pot reduction of potassium permanganate (KMnO4) with L-cysteine (L-cys) as the reducing agent. The procedure is similar to a previous literature with some modifications [38]. The primary solution was prepared using 0.5 g of KMnO4 dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water with continuous stirring, into which 0.2 g of L-cyst was added and maintained at 60 °C for 1 h, yielding a brown precipitate along with a yellow supernatant. The precipitate was collected, thoroughly washed with distilled water, and dried in a hot air oven at 40 °C for 12 h. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 15 min) and further purified via dialysis for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1 kDa. The filtered supernatant solution was deep frozen at −80 °C for 24 h and then subjected to freeze-drying under a reduced pressure of 0.02 mbar. After 72 h of lyophilization, a yellow powder was obtained and stored at ∼ 4 °C for future investigations. Both the solid precipitate and the powder obtained from the supernatant appeared to be stable over several months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA luminescent sensor using Mn3O4 Nanozymes; optimization of assay conditions and sensing studies
ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme kinetic assay and fluorescence studies were conducted by diluting 500 µL (0.4 mg/mL) of QDs with 1 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The enzyme kinetic studies were conducted by adding various concentrations of DTBC into the QDs under alkaline condition and respective absorbance at 415 nm was measured for 120 s. The spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, employing a slit width of 3 nm and an acquisition time of 0.2 s. The crucial parameters of the sensor; including temperature, pH, and incubation period, were systematically optimized by varying each one. Throughout each optimization step, sensing measurements were conducted by combining 300 µL of dopamine (100 µM) with the QDs. The pH of the system was regulated by adjusting the concentration of the precursors of the PBS. Under optimized conditions, QDs (0.4 mg/mL, 1 mL), PBS buffer (pH 8, 1 mL), and various concentrations of DA standard solutions (500 µL) were added to culture tubes. The mixture was thoroughly homogenized and incubated at 50 ⁰C in a water bath. After 2 h, the mixture was removed from the water bath and allowed to cool down to room temperature before the fluorescence measurements. The selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by introducing 100 μM solutions of diverse biomolecules to the probe and recording the corresponding fluorescence spectra. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements of the probe in the absence and presence of analyte were conducted and the lifetime was calculated using double exponential fit. The HOMO – LUMO levels of the probe and analyte, were estimated from the Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurements using a three-electrode set-up. The real-time assay of the proposed sensor was evaluated by introducing varying concentrations of DA to Sprague Dawley rat blood plasma. The blood plasma was collected by centrifugation of blood at 1500 rpm for 10 min. The concentration of the DA in the samples was retrieved using the method of standard addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Mn3O4 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The present study proposes a straightforward one-step method for synthesizing Mn3O4 QDs utilizing KMnO4 and L-cyst as precursors. The reduction of permanganate by L-cyst is a rapid process, leaving minimal residues. Usually, the permanganate reduction ends up in different oxidation states of Mn-based on the synthesis conditions and reactants [39,40]. The crystallographic planes of the synthesised samples were analysed using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD patterns of the as-prepared brown precipitate and yellow sol are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. The peaks in Fig. 1(a) correspond to the (101), (112), (103), (211), (004), (220), (105), (321), (215), and (400) planes of tetragonal Mn3O4 (ICDD card 24–0734). The absence of any impurity or other MnO x phase peaks confirms the formation of pure Mn3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) with high crystallinity. Following a 48 h dialysis of the supernatant, the complete removal of precursor residues was verified through XRD analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 1(b). The QDs extracted from the yellow supernatant solution and the precipitate formed concurrently replicate the compositional features of the QDs. In alkaline conditions, the reduction of permanganate commonly yields MnO2 as the end product [39]. The (II), (III), and (V) oxidation states of Mn are usually unstable under alkaline conditions [41]. In the present synthesis conditions, the addition of L-cyst provided a slightly acidic nature to the reaction mixture. The stabilization of Mn (II) and Mn (III) is possible in an acidic environment. Figure S1 shows the variation in the composition of permanganate reduction products with different concentrations of the precursors. Variations in concentration or the ratio of precursors lead to a different composition or the absence of QDs. Evidently, the chosen synthesis conditions for producing Mn3O4 QDs exhibit distinctive characteristics.

The microstructure and particle size distribution of the QDs were analysed using high resolution transmission electron microscopy. Fig. 1(c) displays the HRTEM image of the QDs after dialysis and lyophilization. The QDs appear predominantly spherical with monodisperse and minimal agglomeration. The average particle size of the QDs, estimated using log-normal function approximation, is 2.19 ± 0.31 nm (Fig. 1(d)), which is in agreement with the data obtained from Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements (Figure S2). The purification process has led to a narrower size distribution (Figure S3). The interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1(e)) corresponds to the (211) plane of Mn3O4, as estimated from the fast Fourier transform/ inverse Fourier transform (FFT/IFT) processing of HRTEM image, which is consistent with standard value (ICDD card 24–0734). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) (Fig. 1(f)) exhibits broad diffused rings, indicating poor crystallinity, as used to be observed for quantum dot materials.

The chemical composition of Mn3O4 QDs was examined using XPS. The XPS spectrum, shown in Fig. 2 (a), displays peaks centered at 641.7 eV and 653.4 eV corresponding to the Mn
2 p
3 / 2
and
2 p
1 / 2
with an observed spin–orbit splitting of 11.7 eV, which confirms the formation of MnO [25]. The peak attributed to Mn
2 p
3 / 2
has been further resolved into two peaks at 640.5 eV and 642.1 eV corresponding to Mn2+ and Mn3+respectively. The intensities of these peaks imply a compositional ratio of 2:1. A broad satellite peak is also observed in agreement with previous literature [25]. Mn
2 p
1 / 2
peak is resolved into two peaks centered at 652.4 eV and 653.4 eV representing Mn2+ and Mn3+ states. The presence of Mn2+ and Mn3+ ions confirm the formation and composition of Mn3O4 as MnO.Mn2O3 [42–44].

FTIR studies were conducted to reveal the surface states and functional groups. Fig. 2(b) shows the FTIR transmittance spectrum of QDs. There are three significant vibrations (477 cm−1, 538 cm−1, and 648 cm−1) in the fingerprint region. The absorption at 477 cm−1 is ascribed to the coupling between Mn—O stretching modes of octahedral and tetrahedral sites [45]. The peak at 518 cm−1 corresponds to the Mn—O distortion vibration in the octahedral environment, and the vibration at 642 cm−1 is the characteristic Mn—O stretching mode at tetrahedral sites. These results additionally validate the formation of Mn3O4 [46,47]. The broad peaks at 1389 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 are —COOH vibrations [38,48] and the peak at 3422 cm−1 owe to the vibrations of N–H bonds [49]. The presence of carboxylic acid and ammine groups on the surface of QDs indicates the functionalization in synchronization with the synthesis process. Consequently, functionalizing Mn3O4 QDs with L-cyst possibly improves its reactivity and biocompatibility.

The optical properties of the QDs were analyzed using UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy and photoluminescence characterisations. The broad UV–Visible absorption spectrum of Mn3O4 QDs (Fig. 3 (a)) is deconvoluted to four distinct Gaussian peaks at 220 nm, 275 nm, 300 nm, and 370 nm. Earlier literature suggests that the absorption at 210-250 nm is attributed to O2–→ Mn2+ and the 250 – 410 nm absorption corresponds to O2–→ Mn3+ charge transfer transitions. The absorption spectrum corroborates the presence of Mn2+ and Mn3+ ions in the QDs [43,46]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum offers additional confirmation of the composition of QDs. Fig. 3(b) displays the room temperature photoluminescence (PL) response of the Mn3O4 NPs and QDs when excited with 360 nm. The PL emission of QDs exhibits a broad peak centered at 440 nm with a stoke shift of 80 nm. The Mn3O4 QDs exhibit a higher luminescence intensity compared to NPs. As shown in Fig. 3(c) the emission peak intensity varies, and the peak position shifts towards longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength changes from 320 nm to 440 nm. The maximum emission intensity is achieved with 370 nm excitation. The excitation dependency is an observable behaviour of QDs caused by quantum size restriction and surface states [50]. The PL quantum yield of QDs was estimated as 3.8 %, using quinine sulphate as the standard, with an average lifetime of 3.91 ns. The PL emission intensity of QDs were measured at various period showed excellent photostability even after 6 months, as represented in the histogram in Fig. 4 d. This stable PL emission may be due to the cysteine ligands used for the synthesis of QDs, which acts as a capping layer, protecting the QDs from oxidation and thereby providing a favourable environment for stable emission [35]. The oxidation inhibition is confirmed through the addition of common reductants such AA and H2O2. The introduction of these reductants has no impact on the luminescence intensity (Figure S4).

Fig. 4(a) and (b) depicts the pH-dependent PL behaviour of the QDs. When the pH was varied from 7 to 3, the PL intensity of QDs declined linearly showing a slight red shift. As in the case of other luminescent quantum dots, the pH-dependent luminescence behaviour of Mn3O4 QDs can be attributed to the surface states. FTIR measurements revealed the presence of numerous carboxyl groups on the QDs. The observed pH dependence of QDs, supports the prevalence of carboxyl groups [51]. Zhang et al. suggest that the displacement reaction between the surface ligands and H+ ions is the reason behind pH-dependent quenching in an acidic pH environment [52]. Under alkaline conditions, the OH-1 ions provide a favourable environment for the coordination between QDs core and surface ligands that promote dispersibility. In excess, the OH-1 can disturb the surface charges and result in flocculation [53]. The behaviour of QDs substantially supports these observations in an alkaline pH. The PL spectrum significantly changes with the formation of a white mass at pH levels above 10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme kinetic studies of Mn3O4 QDs; catechol oxidase mimicking activity
ce:section.ce:para: The catechol-oxidase-like enzymatic behaviour of QDs was investigated in detail using the steady-state kinetic assay. DTBC was used as a chromogenic agent for the entire study. The oxidation of DTBC was accelerated by the QDs, producing the 3,5-di-tert-butyl-o-benzoquinone [30]. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the formation of 3,5-di-tert-butyl-o-benzoquinone resulted in a new UV–Vis absorption peak at 415 nm that became more pronounced over time. The light yellow QDs changed to a deeper tint in colour after being exposed to DTBC for 5 min. This alteration in colour became more pronounced over time (inset in Fig. 5(a)), reaching a saturation without any additional reducing agents. The kinetic parameters were derived from the absorbance at 415 nm in the presence of different concentrations of DTBC. The Michaelis − Menten curves for a different concentration of DTBC followed a hyperbolic path, as shown in Fig. 5(b). From the reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 5(c)), the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m ) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax) were estimated to be 133.19 µM and 69.40 µMs−1, respectively. The low Km value indicates that DTBC and nanozyme have a strong affinity for one another with a reasonable reaction rate, which is better than similar works [30,54,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of Mn3O4 QDs-based nanoplatform for dopamine detection
ce:section.ce:para: A DA sensor was fabricated based on the fluorescence turn-off strategy using the novel Mn3O4 QDs. In the process of sensor development, emphasis was given in establishing and maintaining three critical parameters: the incubation period, pH, and temperature. The fluorescence response was found to vary with the incubation duration. The response reached its maximum after 2 h and then stabilized (Fig. 6 (a)). As a result, the optimal incubation period for DA detection was chosen to be 2 h. The influence of temperature was also investigated. Even though the system gave a response at room temperature, the response increased up to 50 °C and then stabilizes, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The pH level of the sensing media played a pivotal role in sensor performance. The analysis presented in Fig. 6(c) demonstrates that the most favourable outcome was for a pH value 8.0, and this pH was consistently maintained throughout all the subsequent experiments.

The sensing performance of the developed nano-platform for DA detection was systematically evaluated under optimized assay conditions. Fig. 7 (a) shows the fluorescence spectra of QDs in the presence of various analyte concentrations. This emission quenching of QDs in the presence of DA can be analysed using he Stern – Volmer (S-V) equation [56].

was estimated as 2 × 1013 M−1 s−1 using equation (2). Besides the quenching rate, the limit of detection (LoD) was calculated from the Stern-Volmer (S-V) plot and was found to be 18 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9940. These results represent a relatively lower limit of detection with a good linear response compared to similar works as listed in Table 1 .

The capping of QDs with L-cyst, a non-essential amino acid having thiol and amine side chains [61], facilitates interactions under weak alkaline conditions. The oxidized form of DA can undergo nucleophilic addition reactions with various functional groups, including thiol and amine, through Michael addition or Schiff base reactions [62]. Schiff base condensation occurs between the amine functional groups over the QDs and DA-quinones. The hydrophobic Schiff base bonds cause the DA-quinone to polymerize to form uniform polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles in an aqueous solution as shown in Figure S5 (a) [35]. After the addition of DA, the colour of the QDs changed from pale yellow to light brown, then to dark brown, and eventually to black, indicating the formation of PDA (see inset image of the solutions in Fig. 8 (a) [63]. It has previously been reported that under alkaline conditions, DA can readily oxidize into a quinone structure. However, in the presence of QDs in PBS at pH 8, more DA might have been converted into PDA, as demonstrated by Figure S5 (c). DA is an electron donor, and is prone to oxidation by dissolved oxygen to form dopaquinone, which can act as an electron acceptor [64]. Mn3O4 behaves as a biocompatible agent that can act as both a reducing and oxidizing agent [65]. Literatures suggest that Mn2+, as well as Mn3+, have catalytic property rather than oxidizing capability [39]. This could be the reason behind fast formation of PDA in the presence of QDs rather than in the air, as evident from UV–Vis absorption as shown in Fig. 8(a). The variation in absorbance with the amount of QDs is shown in Fig. 8(b). The increase in absorption suggests the formation of more PDA NPs which is a clear but another indirect indication of catalytic behaviour of the QDs [35]. The formation of PDA particles was apparent in FTIR spectroscopy studies. The peaks at 1367 cm−1 [66], 1534 cm−1 [67], and 1646 cm−1 [68] as shown in Fig. 8(c) can be assigned to C—N—C stretching bonds, C—N bending vibrations of indolequinone structure and C
=
C stretching vibrations present in PDA, respectively.

Nanospheres of PDA are frequently encountered as fluorescence quenchers for a range of dyes, achieved via either energy transfer, electron transfer or complex formation [69,70]. From Fig. 8(d), it is evident that there is no considerable overlap between the fluorescence emission and absorption of the probe and analyte, eliminating possibility of FRET between QDs and PDA. Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of QDs and PDA were estimated to explore the possibility of charge transfer between them. The calculations were performed using the onset oxidation (
V
ox
) and reduction potentials (
V
red
) from the cyclic voltammetry, along with the optical bandgap (
E g
) derived from UV–Vis absorption spectra, as described elsewhere [71]. The following empirical formulae have been used to estimate the HOMO and LUMO levels:

Fig. 9 (a) and (b) present the CV curves for QDs and PDA, respectively. The Tauc plots for each material are provided in their respective insets.
V
ox
for QDs is obtained as 0.36 V and the corresponding HOMO is −4.76 eV. LUMO level is at −1.33 eV. Similarly, for PDA
V
red
is −0.32 V and the HOMO and LUMO levels are at −4.08 eV and −2.07 eV, respectively. These values are comparable with previously reports [72,73]. A considerable overlap exists between LUMO levels of QDs and PDA, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The earlier reports suggest that PDA can form donor–acceptor pairs with different entities and promotes electron transfer between donor–acceptor pairs [74]. The change in the fluorescence lifetime of the probe, induced by the presence of the quencher, corroborates with the earlier observations (Figure S5 (b)).

When the fluorophore and the analyte become attached via Schiff base/Michael addition reaction, electron transfer between LUMO levels of the donor and acceptors is an excellent likelihood. This eventually leads to the formation of a charge transfer complex between the QDs and PDA, restraining the radiative emission of QDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. The zeta potential of QDs was increased from –23.1 mV to −56.7 mV upon the addition of DA, suggesting that the complex formed was stable in aqueous form without aggregation (Figure S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating interferences on the proposed DA sensor using Mn3O4 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of Mn3O4 QDs towards DA was evaluated in the presence of various potential interfering metal ions and biomolecules. Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the change in fluorescence intensity of QDs in the presence of 100 µM of different analytes. The presence of Fe3+ ions causes a decline in fluorescence intensity, though it is much less pronounced than the quenching induced by DA. This behaviour can be attributed to the redox reaction between cysteine and Fe3+ ions. To counteract this potential background interference of Fe3+ ions, 3 mM oxalic acid was introduced as a masking reagent while sensing DA from an environment containing Fe3+ or from an unknown environment. Fig. 10(b) shows that QDs do show a potential PL quenching behaviour for catechol, which is the parent atom of the catecholamine family. This broadens the application of QDs as a common indicator for the catecholamine family. The reliability of the proposed sensor in a real-time matrix was tested using DA spiked in Sprague Dawley (SD) rat blood plasma and was detected using the standard addition method. The corresponding results presented in Table 2 show a recovery rate between 91 % to 102 %. This indicates that the proposed sensing platform is reliable for detecting DA in a real matrix.

Luminescent Mn3O4 QDs were synthesized through a novel single-step permanganate reduction process. The L-cyst assisted synthesis method resulted in a gentle reaction process, allowing distinctive surface functionalization with ammine coating yielding remarkable photostability. The surface functionalization enables the proper attachment of DA and QDs via the Schiff base/Michael addition reaction, and facilitates the polymerization of DA to PDA. The formation of PDA results in an electron donor–acceptor pair between QDs and PDA, leading to the formation of a charge transfer complex, which effectively quenches the fluorescence. This mechanism was employed for the subsequent development of highly sensitive and selective optical DA sensors with LoD of 18 nM. Remarkably, QDs also demonstrated superior activity similar to that of the enzyme catechol oxidase, with the kinetic parameters of 133.19 µM and 69.40 µMs−1 as Km and Vmax respectively. The straightforward and distinctive synthesis pathway of QDs, exhibiting superior enzymatic activity and direct applicability in DA sensing, offers critical insights for healthcare and disease diagnostics. This novel approach can be extended to the development of point-of-care sensors, bio-labelling, and drug development.
